title: Per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels
caption: Across the globe in 2025, per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels spans a wide range, measured in kilowatt-hours per person using a continuous color scale from 0 to 10,000 kWh. The highest values, falling in the 5,000 to 10,000 kWh band, appear in the United States, Canada, Australia, Russia, and Saudi Arabia. Intermediate levels, between 1,000 and 5,000 kWh, cover much of Europe including Germany, France, and the United Kingdom, as well as China, South Korea, and parts of South America like Argentina and Brazil. Lower generation, in the 200 to 1,000 kWh range, includes India, Mexico, Indonesia, and several North African nations such as Egypt and Morocco. The lowest visible band, 0 to 200 kWh, dominates sub-Saharan Africa — including Nigeria, Ethiopia, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo — along with countries in Central Asia like Tajikistan and Kyrgyzstan. Several regions show no data, marked by hatched patterns, including Greenland, Western Sahara, and small island states in the Pacific and Caribbean. The geographic distribution shows higher fossil-fuel-based electricity generation concentrated in industrialized economies and major energy exporters, while lower generation aligns with regions having less developed grid infrastructure or greater reliance on non-fossil sources. The most visible pattern is the concentration of the highest per capita fossil fuel electricity generation in North America, Australia, and the Gulf states, contrasted with widespread low generation across sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia.
